GB1-W06-A1 - Ground Headlock Escape Push.body:
## Concept

# Ground Headlock Escape Pushing Head Away + Armbar

**Position:** Side Control / Ground Headlock  
**Category:** Defense (Self-Defense & Sport)  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 6 A1  
**Confidence:** 4/5

Against a ground headlock (common when opponent controls from side or after a takedown), you create strong frames with your arms and elbows to generate space, push their head away to break the grip or angle, insert your knee between their legs while keeping your head high to avoid chokes or gift-wrap, and either recover guard or finish with an armbar on the over-wrapped arm. This is the core GB1 Week 6 ground headlock escape that bridges turtle recovery and side control defense.

GB1 emphasizes the same frame + elbow space + head high principles taught for turtle recoveries (A2/A3) and back bridge escapes (B2/B3), with the added finish option of clearing the head and attacking the arm.

## Setup

You are on your back or side in a ground headlock (opponent's arm wrapped around your head/neck from side control or after you turtled and they took the head). Their weight is heavy on you or they are flattening you.

- Goal: Create immediate space with frames, break the headlock angle by pushing their head, insert knee between legs, keep head safe (high/outside), and either recover full guard or isolate the arm for armbar.
- Direct connection to all W06 turtle and back escape work.

Wikilink connections:
- Core entry point for the full Week 6 turtle/back family: see [[GB1-W06-A2 - Recover Guard from Rear Turtle Position]], [[GB1-W06-A3 - Recover Guard from Front Turtle Position]], [[GB1-W06-B1 - Headlock Escape Taking the Back + Shoulder Lock]], [[GB1-W06-B2 - Bridge Escape from the Back + Side Mount]], [[GB1-W06-B3 - Bridge Escape from the Back Avoiding the Mount + Recover Guard]].
- Builds on Week 2 turtle entry [[GB1-W02-B2 - Escape Side Mount to Turtle Position]] and the ground headlock escape progression in [[GB1-W14-A1 - Escape Ground Headlock to Turtle + Inversion + Technical Mount + Straight Armbar]].
- Shares headlock bridge mechanics with [[GB1-W04-A2 - Escape Mount with Headlock Using Bridge]].

## Execution

### Create the Frame + Space (Immediate Priority)
1. As the headlock lands or tightens, immediately flare your elbows and create a strong two-arm frame against their chest, shoulder, or hip. Do not let them flatten you or sink the grip.
2. Use the frames to generate space between your head/neck and their controlling arm/shoulder.

### Push the Head Away + Break the Angle
1. With the frame established, use your hands/forearms to push or frame against their head (chin or side of face), driving it away from your body. This breaks their leverage and creates the angle to insert your knee or recover posture.
2. Keep your own head high and outside their chest/shoulder line — never let it get trapped inside or under their armpit (avoids choke/gift-wrap).

### Insert Knee + Hip Drive to Recover or Finish
1. Once space is created, insert your near knee between their legs (the same mechanics as A2/A3 turtle recoveries).
2. Drive your hips up and laterally (to the side) while posting on your frames and free leg. Use body weight through your base, not just arm strength.
3. Option A: Continue the hip drive to recover full guard (see A2/A3 details).
4. Option B (when arm is exposed): Clear your head fully, trap their attacking arm (elbow pin or figure-4), and finish with armbar by extending your hips and controlling the wrist.

### Hand Placement & References
- **Elbow flare + two-arm frame**: The foundation. Elbows out creates the wedge that prevents flattening and buys the time to push the head.
- **Head push / frame on their head**: Specific to A1 — drives their posture back and opens the arm for the finish or the knee insertion.
- **Head high/outside**: Non-negotiable. Drop your head inside and you give them the choke or gift-wrap control.
- **Knee between legs + lateral hip drive**: Same as the core W06 turtle recovery (FM6QISFsZeU). Weight through the posted leg and frames.
- **Arm isolation for finish**: Once head is clear, immediate elbow trap or wrist control on the over-wrapped arm for the armbar.

## Common Mistakes
- Dropping your head inside their armpit or chest (immediate choke/gift-wrap threat).
- Weak or collapsed frames (they flatten you and sink the headlock).
- Pushing only with arms instead of using elbow wedge + hip drive together.
- Forgetting the knee insertion (you stay flat and they maintain control).
- Rushing the armbar before the head is fully clear (they can roll or re-grip).
- Poor base during hip drive (you bridge straight up and they base out).

## Key Principles
- **Strong arm frame + elbow flare for space (FM6QISFsZeU / GB W06)**: Create the frame first with elbows wide before any head push or hip drive. This is the same opening move for all turtle and back escapes in Week 6.
- **Push the head away to break the angle**: Specific A1 detail — the head push creates the posture break that lets you insert the knee or isolate the arm. Do not just pull your own head out; actively drive theirs.
- **Head position high and outside to avoid chokes**: Keep your chin tucked but head elevated and angled away from their chest/shoulder. This denies gift-wrap and chokes while you work the escape or finish.
- **Knee between the legs + lateral hip drive**: Identical to A2/A3 recoveries. Insert the knee, then drive hips up and to the side using legs and frames for base. Weight distribution through the base leg, not upper body.
- **Safe transition with underhook or collar tie control**: During the drive, establish an underhook or collar tie so you do not expose your back or neck on the way to guard or the armbar finish.
- **Armbar finish only after head clearance**: The arm is attacked only once your head is free and their posture is broken. Trap the elbow first, then extend.
- **Consistent GB Week 6 language**: Frame/elbow space, head high, knee insertion, lateral hip drive, and safe control are the unifying cues across A1–B3 and tie directly to the turtle entries you already drilled in Week 2.

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate / Curriculum Priority (per GB Greensboro rule):**
- Primary: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FM6QISFsZeU — Deep reviewed for the exact frame + elbow space + knee insertion + head high + lateral hip drive sequence that makes the ground headlock escape work. GB Week 6 curriculum context confirmed via gbcentennial.com/gracie-barra-week-6-take-the-back-and-turtle-guard/ and institute.graciebarra.com/blog/guard-recover-from-turtle-position.
- Supporting: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PYk5Ei2IZBk — Back engagement framing and safe transitions from headlock control.
- Cross-reference the full W06 family videos and the Harrogate/Scottsdale headlock mechanics from Wave 2.7 (FXW6LE_6wQ4, ODF7dH8DjIg) for the frame/drive family consistency.

These are the highest-fidelity public GB-aligned and curriculum sources for the Week 6 ground headlock and turtle recovery mechanics. All cards in this cluster (A1–B3) share the same core cues.

Cross-references: Drill A1 together with the turtle recoveries (A2/A3) and the back bridge escapes (B2/B3). The head push + armbar finish is the unique A1 option; the frame + knee + hip drive is universal. Then drill the counters from side control and mount escapes you already know (W04 family).

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- Strong two-arm frame with elbows flared against headlock pressure.
- Head push / frame on opponent's head creating the angle.
- Knee insertion between legs with head high.
- Lateral hip drive to guard or armbar setup.
- Contrast: head dropped inside (bad) vs high/outside (good).
- Arm isolation and hip extension for the finish.

**Hand Placement & References**  
The elbow flare frame, active head push to break posture, continuous head high/outside position, and knee-between-legs insertion with lateral hip drive (weight through base) are the non-negotiable details extracted from the GB W06 sources (FM6QISFsZeU primary + curriculum pages). These unify the entire Week 6 back/turtle/ground headlock family and connect directly to the frame/drive work from Week 2 (Harrogate) and mount escapes (Week 4). Drill the full sequence: frame → head push → knee + hip drive → guard or armbar. Personal Cues & Notes

GB1-W06-A2 - Recover Guard from Rear Tur.body:
## Concept

# Recover Guard from Rear Turtle Position

**Position:** Turtle (Rear Control)  
**Category:** Guard Recovery  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 6 A2  
**Confidence:** 4/5

From rear turtle (opponent on your back, controlling your hips or upper body), you create a strong frame with your arms and elbows to generate space, insert your knee between their legs while keeping your head high to avoid chokes or gift-wrap, and drive your hips up and laterally to recover full guard or technical stand-up. This is the fundamental GB1 Week 6 turtle recovery that every student must own.

GB1 teaches this with precise posture: frames first (never strength), head safety (high and outside), knee path between the legs (not over), and lateral hip drive using your legs and base rather than upper body.

## Setup

You are in turtle position. Opponent has taken your back or is riding you from behind (hooks in or knees on the mat, upper body control). They are attempting to flatten you or set up a choke (rear naked, gift-wrap, etc.).

- Goal: Immediate frames to stop flattening, create space, insert knee between their legs, keep head safe, and use hip drive to recover closed guard or stand and face them.
- Direct follow-up to the turtle entries you drilled in Week 2 (W02-B2/B3).

Wikilink connections:
- Core of the Week 6 family: [[GB1-W06-A1 - Ground Headlock Escape Pushing Head Away + Armbar]], [[GB1-W06-A3 - Recover Guard from Front Turtle Position]], [[GB1-W06-B1 - Headlock Escape Taking the Back + Shoulder Lock]], [[GB1-W06-B2 - Bridge Escape from the Back + Side Mount]], [[GB1-W06-B3 - Bridge Escape from the Back Avoiding the Mount + Recover Guard]].
- Builds directly on [[GB1-W02-B2 - Escape Side Mount to Turtle Position]] and [[GB1-W02-B3 - Double Leg Takedown from Turtle Position]] (the entries that put you here).
- Cross-ref to [[GB1-W09-A1 - Block Punches from Turtle Position + Recover Guard]] for the punch-defense variation.

## Execution

### Create Strong Frames + Space (Stop the Flatten)
1. Immediately bring your arms into a strong frame — forearms or elbows flared against their chest, hip, or thigh. Create a wedge that prevents them from driving their weight down and flattening your hips to the mat.
2. Elbows wide and active. This buys the time and space for the next steps.

### Insert the Knee Between the Legs
1. With the frame holding space, slide or insert your near knee between their legs (the critical path). Do not go over the top of their leg or hip — that allows them to turn and keep the back.
2. Keep your head high and outside their shoulder/chest line the entire time. Chin tucked, eyes up — this denies the rear naked choke and gift-wrap.

### Lateral Hip Drive to Recover Guard
1. Post on your frames and the free leg. Drive your hips up and strongly to the side (lateral direction toward the framed side).
2. Use your legs and base for the power — weight distribution through the posted leg and frames, not just arm push. This rolls you to your hip and allows guard recovery or technical stand-up.
3. As you recover, establish an underhook or collar tie on their far arm/shoulder so you do not give them easy access to your neck or back on the way up.
4. Finish in closed guard (or half guard if they are fast) facing them, or stand and re-engage in good posture.

### Hand Placement & References
- **Two-arm frame with elbow flare**: The opening move. Wide elbows create the wedge that stops their weight and creates the gap for the knee.
- **Head high/outside (constant)**: The safety cue. Never let your head drop inside their arm or chest. This single detail defeats most chokes from the back.
- **Knee between the legs**: The mechanical key for the recovery path. Inside path lets you roll to guard; over-the-top path keeps you turtled or gives them the back again.
- **Lateral hip drive + weight through base**: Hips up and to the side, power from legs and posted frames. This is what actually moves you to guard.
- **Underhook/collar tie on the way up**: Safe transition control. Do not recover guard or stand while leaving your neck or back exposed.

## Common Mistakes
- Weak or collapsed frames (they flatten you instantly and set up the choke).
- Dropping your head inside (immediate RNC or gift-wrap).
- Knee over the leg/hip instead of between (you stay turtled or they keep the back).
- Bridging straight up instead of lateral drive (they base out and you stay flat).
- Using only upper body strength instead of legs + base (gasses you, no recovery).
- Recovering without underhook/collar tie (they take your back again or choke on the way).
- Poor head position during the drive (they sink the choke as you roll).

## Key Principles
- **Strong arm frame + elbow flare for space (FM6QISFsZeU / GB W06)**: Frames with elbows wide are the first and most important action. They stop the flatten and create the gap for everything else. Same opening as A1 ground headlock and B2/B3 bridge escapes.
- **Head position high and outside to avoid chokes**: Keep your head elevated and angled away from their chest/shoulder line at all times. This is the universal safety cue for all W06 back and turtle work.
- **Insert knee between the legs, not over**: The inside path is the difference between recovering guard and staying turtled or giving them the back. Direct from the GB W06 instruction.
- **Lateral hip drive up and to the side**: Not a straight bridge. Drive hips up + laterally using your legs and base. This rolls you to the guard position.
- **Weight through legs and frames, not arms**: Power comes from posting and driving with the lower body. Upper body frames only create space and base.
- **Safe transition with underhook or collar tie**: Establish control on their far side as you recover so the transition itself does not give them a submission or the back.
- **Consistent GB Week 6 turtle recovery language**: Frame/elbow space, head high/outside, knee between legs, lateral hip drive, weight through base, and safe control unify A1–B3 and connect to the Week 2 turtle entries and all back escapes.

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate / Curriculum Priority (per GB Greensboro rule):**
- Primary: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FM6QISFsZeU — Deep reviewed for the exact frame + elbow space + knee-between-legs + head high + lateral hip drive sequence. Official GB Week 6 curriculum alignment via https://gbcentennial.com/gracie-barra-week-6-take-the-back-and-turtle-guard/ and https://institute.graciebarra.com/blog/guard-recover-from-turtle-position (detailed steps for frames, hip insertion, recovery paths).
- Supporting: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PYk5Ei2IZBk — Posture, head position, and frame/drive with back engagement for safe transitions.
- Additional GB-aligned: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Ng8j1S67plw and https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zBbF78VpRNw for variations and headlock context.

These are the highest-fidelity public GB curriculum sources for the Week 6 rear turtle recovery. All W06 cards share the core cues.

Cross-references: Drill A2 together with A1 (ground headlock), A3 (front turtle), and B2/B3 (bridge escapes). Then connect to the entries from Week 2 (W02-B2/B3) and the punch defense turtle recovery (W09-A1). The full loop: entry to turtle → recovery to guard or back take (B1).

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- Strong two-arm frame with elbows flared in rear turtle (prevent flatten).
- Head high/outside position (safety).
- Knee inserting between opponent's legs.
- Lateral hip drive (up + side arrow) with base posting.
- Recovery to closed guard with underhook control.
- Contrast: head dropped inside (choke) vs high/outside (safe).

**Hand Placement & References**  
Elbow-flared arm frames to stop flattening and create space, constant head high/outside position, knee path strictly between the legs, lateral (not straight) hip drive powered by legs and posted base, and underhook/collar tie for safe transition are the non-negotiable details from the GB W06 sources (FM6QISFsZeU primary + gbcentennial + institute.graciebarra.com detailed recovery paths). These are identical to the cues used in A1, A3, B1–B3 and tie directly to the frame/drive family from Week 2 Harrogate/Scottsdale work. Drill the full W06 cluster as one curriculum block: frame → knee insertion → lateral hip drive → safe guard or back control.

## Personal Cues & Notes

GB1-W06-A3 - Recover Guard from Front Tu.body:
## Concept

# Recover Guard from Front Turtle Position

**Position:** Turtle (Front Control)  
**Category:** Guard Recovery  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 6 A3  
**Confidence:** 4/5

From front turtle (opponent in front of you or controlling your head/shoulders from a front headlock or chest-on-chest turtle), you use the same strong arm frames and elbow flare to create space, insert your knee between their legs while keeping your head high to avoid chokes or gift-wrap, and drive hips up and laterally to recover guard or technical stand-up. The front angle requires slight adaptations in frame placement and hip direction but the core cues are identical to A2.

GB1 Week 6 teaches front and rear turtle recoveries together as the unified "turtle escape" curriculum block.

## Setup

You are turtled. Opponent has front head control, a front headlock, or is chest-to-chest / shoulder-on-chest preventing you from opening guard. They may be setting up a choke (guillotine, gift-wrap from front) or flattening you.

- Goal: Immediate frames to create space and stop the flatten, insert knee between legs, head high/outside, lateral hip drive to recover closed guard or stand and face them safely.
- Direct counterpart to A2 rear turtle recovery.

Wikilink connections:
- Part of the complete Week 6 turtle/back family: [[GB1-W06-A1 - Ground Headlock Escape Pushing Head Away + Armbar]], [[GB1-W06-A2 - Recover Guard from Rear Turtle Position]], [[GB1-W06-B1 - Headlock Escape Taking the Back + Shoulder Lock]], [[GB1-W06-B2 - Bridge Escape from the Back + Side Mount]], [[GB1-W06-B3 - Bridge Escape from the Back Avoiding the Mount + Recover Guard]].
- Builds on [[GB1-W02-B2 - Escape Side Mount to Turtle Position]] and connects to [[GB1-W09-A1 - Block Punches from Turtle Position + Recover Guard]] (front turtle punch defense variation).
- Shares elbow frame + hip drive principles with [[GB1-W04-B2 - Escape Mount with Elbows (Opponent Won't Open Knee) + Recover Full Guard]].

## Execution

### Create Strong Frames + Space (Front Angle)
1. Bring your arms into a strong frame — elbows flared against their chest, shoulder, or hip. In front turtle the frames may be more vertical or against their near shoulder/chest. Create the wedge that prevents them from driving you flat or sinking a front headlock.
2. Elbows active and wide. This is the same opening as A2 but adjusted for the front angle.

### Insert Knee + Head Safety
1. With frames holding space, insert your knee between their legs (same critical inside path as A2). Do not go over their hip.
2. Keep your head high and outside their shoulder/chest line. In front control this often means driving your head up into their armpit or to the side to deny the guillotine or gift-wrap. Chin tucked, posture tall.

### Lateral Hip Drive + Safe Transition
1. Post on frames and free leg. Drive hips up and to the side (lateral direction that opens your guard or allows technical stand-up).
2. Power from legs and base. Weight through the posted leg and frames.
3. Establish underhook or collar tie on their far arm as you recover so the transition is safe (no back exposure, no easy choke on the roll).
4. Recover to closed guard facing them or stand in good posture.

### Hand Placement & References
- **Elbow-flared frame (front-adapted)**: Same wedge principle as A2 but often against their near side or chest. Stops the flatten and creates the gap.
- **Head high/outside (front specific)**: Drive head up into their armpit or laterally away. Denies front chokes and gift-wrap. Same safety cue as all W06 work.
- **Knee between legs**: Universal W06 cue. Inside path is required for guard recovery.
- **Lateral hip drive + base weight**: Identical power source as A2. Hips up + side, legs and frames do the work.
- **Underhook/collar tie control**: Safe transition. Critical when recovering from front control (they can spin to your back easily).

## Common Mistakes
- Collapsed frames (front headlock sinks or they flatten you).
- Head dropped or pulled inside (guillotine or gift-wrap immediate).
- Knee over the hip (they keep front control or you stay flat).
- Straight-up bridge instead of lateral drive (they base and you make no progress).
- No underhook on recovery (they take your back on the roll).
- Leaning too far forward with upper body (they use your momentum).

## Key Principles
- **Strong arm frame + elbow flare for space (FM6QISFsZeU / GB W06)**: The opening move is identical in principle to A2. Frames first, elbows wide, create the wedge. Front angle just changes the exact contact points.
- **Head position high and outside (front-adapted)**: Drive your head up or laterally into their armpit/shoulder to deny chokes. Same universal safety cue for the entire W06 family.
- **Knee between the legs, not over**: The mechanical key. Inside path lets you roll to guard; over path keeps you turtled under front pressure.
- **Lateral hip drive up and to the side**: Same as A2. Up + lateral using legs and base. This is what actually recovers the guard from front turtle.
- **Weight through legs and frames**: Power from the lower body and posted base. Upper body only frames and directs.
- **Safe transition with underhook/collar tie**: Even more critical from front turtle — they can easily take your back during a sloppy recovery. Control their far side.
- **Consistent GB Week 6 language**: Frame/elbow space, head high/outside, knee between legs, lateral hip drive, weight through base, safe control. Unifies A1–B3 and connects front turtle directly to the rear turtle (A2) and bridge escapes (B2/B3).

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate / Curriculum Priority (per GB Greensboro rule):**
- Primary: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FM6QISFsZeU — Deep reviewed for the core sequence (frame + elbow space + knee insertion + head high + lateral hip drive). Applies to both rear and front turtle with the angle adaptations noted. GB Week 6 curriculum confirmed on gbcentennial.com (take the back and turtle guard) and institute.graciebarra.com/blog/guard-recover-from-turtle-position (detailed frame/hip insertion/recovery paths for front and rear).
- Supporting: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PYk5Ei2IZBk and https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Ng8j1S67plw for posture and transition details.

Cross-references: Drill A3 together with A2 (rear) as the paired turtle recoveries. Then connect to A1 (ground headlock), B1 (headlock to back take), and B2/B3 (bridge escapes). Full loop with Week 2 entries (W02-B2/B3) and W09-A1 (block punches turtle).

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- Elbow frame in front turtle (vertical or chest contact).
- Head driving high into armpit or laterally (safety vs guillotine).
- Knee insertion between legs from front angle.
- Lateral hip drive recovering to guard.
- Underhook established during the roll.
- Contrast images: head inside (bad) vs high (good).

**Hand Placement & References**  
Elbow-flared frames (front-adapted contact), head high/outside drive (into armpit or side), strict knee-between-legs path, lateral hip drive powered by legs/base, and immediate underhook/collar tie for safe transition are the details from the GB W06 sources. These are the same cues as A2 with front-angle adjustments and unify the entire Week 6 cluster. Drill A2 + A3 as a paired recovery block, then the full A1–B3 family.

## Personal Cues & Notes

GB1-W06-B1 - Headlock Escape Taking the.body:
## Concept

# Headlock Escape Taking the Back + Shoulder Lock

**Position:** Side Control / Headlock  
**Category:** Submission (from Escape)  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 6 B1  
**Confidence:** 4/5

From a headlock (ground or side control), you use strong frames and elbow space to create room, keep your head high to stay safe, and execute a reversal or roll that lets you take their back. Once on the back, finish with a shoulder lock (or armbar) using the same control principles. This is the "escape and punish" option in the Week 6 back/headlock curriculum.

GB1 teaches the headlock escape as part of the unified turtle/back recovery family (A1–B3), with the back take as the high-percentage finish when the opponent over-commits or leaves the back exposed during your recovery.

## Setup

You are in a ground headlock, side headlock, or front headlock (opponent's arm wrapped, weight on you or controlling your posture). They may be setting up a choke or strike.

- Goal: Frame to create space and safety, use head high position and hip drive or roll to reverse or circle to their back, establish seatbelt or underhook control, and finish with shoulder lock (or armbar) from the back.
- Direct connection to all W06 back/turtle work and the standing headlock escapes from Week 2.

Wikilink connections:
- Part of the complete Week 6 family: [[GB1-W06-A1 - Ground Headlock Escape Pushing Head Away + Armbar]], [[GB1-W06-A2 - Recover Guard from Rear Turtle Position]], [[GB1-W06-A3 - Recover Guard from Front Turtle Position]], [[GB1-W06-B2 - Bridge Escape from the Back + Side Mount]], [[GB1-W06-B3 - Bridge Escape from the Back Avoiding the Mount + Recover Guard]].
- Builds on the standing headlock escape and back take mechanics in [[GB1-W02-A1 - Escape Side Standing Headlock + Rear Takedown + Technical Mount + Armbar]] and the turtle back take in [[GB1-W12-A2 - Take the Back from Turtle + Seatbelt Grip]].
- Shares frame/drive and technical mount concepts with [[GB1-W04-A1 - Double Lapel Grab Escape + Outside Hook Takedown + Straight Armbar]].

## Execution

### Frame + Head Safety (Create the Opportunity)
1. Immediate strong frame with arms and elbows flared to create space and stop the headlock from sinking or flattening you.
2. Keep your head high and outside their chest/shoulder. Chin tucked. This is the safety that lets you work the escape without getting choked.

### Escape + Take the Back
1. Use the space from the frames and a hip drive, bridge, or roll (depending on angle) to circle or reverse to their back. The exact path often mirrors the lateral hip drive from A2/A3 or the bridge escapes in B2/B3.
2. As you arrive on their back, establish seatbelt grip (one arm under their armpit, the other over the shoulder or across the chest) or strong underhook + collar tie control. Do not leave your own neck exposed.
3. Keep your head safe (high or on their back) during the transition.

### Finish with Shoulder Lock (or Armbar)
1. Once the back is secured, isolate their near arm (the one that was headlocking you or the far arm depending on grip).
2. Use a figure-4 or elbow trap, extend your hips, and apply the shoulder lock (or transition to armbar). Control their posture with your legs/hooks so they cannot roll out or stand.
3. Finish with pressure through your hips and chest, not just arm strength.

### Hand Placement & References
- **Elbow flare frame**: Creates the initial space and safety to begin the reversal/roll to the back.
- **Head high/outside throughout**: Denies their choke and keeps you safe while you take their back.
- **Seatbelt or underhook + collar tie on arrival**: The control grip that lets you finish without them escaping or reversing on you.
- **Arm isolation + hip extension for shoulder lock**: Trap the elbow, extend hips, chest pressure. Same leverage principles as other GB armbars.
- **Hooks/legs for posture control**: Prevent them from standing or rolling during the finish.

## Common Mistakes
- Weak frames (headlock sinks or you get flattened before you can move).
- Dropping head inside during the escape (they choke you or you lose the back take).
- Poor seatbelt/underhook on arrival (they roll you off or stand).
- Rushing the shoulder lock before back control is solid (they escape or reverse).
- No hooks/leg control during finish (they stand or roll).
- Using only arm strength for the lock (they posture out).

## Key Principles
- **Strong arm frame + elbow flare for space (FM6QISFsZeU / GB W06)**: The opening that creates the opportunity. Same as A1–A3 and B2/B3. Frames first, then move.
- **Head position high and outside**: Universal safety cue for the entire W06 family. Lets you work the escape and back take without giving them the choke.
- **Take the back as the high-percentage finish**: When the opponent over-commits on the headlock or leaves their back exposed during your recovery, circling or rolling to the back is often easier and higher percentage than pure guard recovery.
- **Seatbelt/underhook + collar tie control on arrival**: Do not just "get to the back" — arrive with control so the finish is available and safe.
- **Shoulder lock / armbar from back with hip extension + posture control**: Leverage over strength. Trap the elbow, extend hips, use your legs/hooks to keep their posture broken.
- **Consistent GB Week 6 + Week 2 language**: Frame space, head high, hip drive/roll to back, seatbelt control, and technical finishes tie this directly to the standing headlock work (Harrogate FXW6LE_6wQ4) and the turtle back take (W12-A2).
- **Reaction-based reversal**: Use their headlock pressure against them — the harder they drive the headlock, the more space your frames create for the back take.

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate / Curriculum Priority (per GB Greensboro rule):**
- Primary: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FM6QISFsZeU — Frame + space + head high + hip drive mechanics that enable the headlock escape and safe back take. GB Week 6 curriculum (gbcentennial.com + institute.graciebarra.com turtle/back content).
- Supporting: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PYk5Ei2IZBk — Back engagement framing and safe transitions to back control.
- Cross-ref the standing headlock escape and back take from Wave 2.7 (FXW6LE_6wQ4 Harrogate Week 2 + ODF7dH8DjIg Scottsdale roll reversal) for the frame/drive and reaction reversal family.

Cross-references: Drill B1 with the full W06 cluster (A1–B3). The headlock escape to back take is the "punish" option when they over-commit. Connect to Week 2 standing headlock (W02-A1) and W12-A2 turtle back take for the complete back-take curriculum.

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- Frame + elbow flare in headlock (space creation).
- Head high during the reversal/roll to back.
- Seatbelt grip established on arrival.
- Shoulder lock finish (elbow trap + hip extension).
- Contrast: head inside (choke risk) vs high (safe back take).

**Hand Placement & References**  
Elbow flare frames for space and safety, constant head high/outside, seatbelt or strong underhook + collar tie on back arrival, and elbow trap + hip extension for the shoulder lock are the details from the GB W06 sources. These unify the headlock escape (B1) with the turtle recoveries (A1–A3) and bridge escapes (B2/B3), and connect to the Week 2 standing headlock family. Drill the full escape-to-back-take-to-finish sequence.

## Personal Cues & Notes

GB1-W06-B2 - Bridge Escape from the Back.body:
## Concept

# Bridge Escape from the Back + Side Mount

**Position:** Back Control  
**Category:** Defense  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 6 B2  
**Confidence:** 4/5

From back control (opponent has your back, hooks in or riding), you create strong frames with arms and elbows to generate space and stop flattening, keep your head high to avoid chokes, bridge explosively while driving hips to the side, and recover to side mount (or guard). This is the direct back-control counterpart to the mount bridge escapes (W04-A2/A3/B2) and the turtle recoveries (A2/A3).

GB1 Week 6 unifies the back bridge escape with the turtle and ground headlock work under the same mechanical cues.

## Setup

You are on your back or side with opponent controlling from the back (seatbelt, hooks, or upper body ride). They are attempting to sink a choke (RNC, gift-wrap, etc.) or flatten you for control.

- Goal: Immediate frames to create space and protect the neck, explosive bridge with lateral hip drive, recover to side mount (chest-to-chest or hip-to-hip) or guard while maintaining head safety.
- Direct extension of the mount bridge escapes and turtle recoveries.

Wikilink connections:
- Core of the Week 6 back/turtle family: [[GB1-W06-A1 - Ground Headlock Escape Pushing Head Away + Armbar]], [[GB1-W06-A2 - Recover Guard from Rear Turtle Position]], [[GB1-W06-A3 - Recover Guard from Front Turtle Position]], [[GB1-W06-B1 - Headlock Escape Taking the Back + Shoulder Lock]], [[GB1-W06-B3 - Bridge Escape from the Back Avoiding the Mount + Recover Guard]].
- Shares bridge + frame + hip drive principles with [[GB1-W04-A2 - Escape Mount with Headlock Using Bridge]], [[GB1-W04-A3 - Escape Mount with Choke Using Bridge]], and [[GB1-W04-B2 - Escape Mount with Elbows (Opponent Won't Open Knee) + Recover Full Guard]].
- Connects to side mount defense and KOB escapes (W04 family).

## Execution

### Frame + Head Safety (Stop the Choke/Flatten)
1. Immediate strong two-arm frame with elbows flared against their chest, arm, or hip. Create the wedge that prevents them from sinking the choke or flattening your hips.
2. Head high and outside their shoulder/chest line. Chin tucked. Deny the RNC and gift-wrap.

### Explosive Bridge + Lateral Hip Drive
1. With frames holding space, bridge explosively (big hips up and slightly angled, same as mount headlock bridge escapes).
2. Simultaneously drive hips laterally to the side (the direction that lets you roll to side mount or guard). Use legs and base for power.
3. As you roll, use the frames to clear their arm or hook and arrive chest-to-chest or hip-to-hip in side mount (underhook or neck control, knees tight).
4. Option: Continue the drive to recover full guard if the angle allows (see A2/A3).

### Safe Arrival and Control
1. On arrival in side mount, immediately establish underhook or collar tie and tight knees so they cannot easily recover guard or reverse.
2. Head remains high and safe throughout.

### Hand Placement & References
- **Elbow flare frame**: Stops the choke/flatten and creates the space for the bridge and roll.
- **Head high/outside**: Universal W06 safety. Never drop inside during the bridge.
- **Explosive bridge + lateral hip drive**: Hips up and to the side (not straight up). Power from legs + base. Same as mount escapes (W04) and turtle recoveries (A2/A3).
- **Frame clearance on the roll**: Use the frames to push their arm or hook out of the way as you arrive in side mount.
- **Underhook + tight knees on arrival**: Secure the position so the escape actually sticks.

## Common Mistakes
- Weak frames (choke sinks or they flatten you before the bridge).
- Head drops inside during bridge (choke or they keep the back).
- Bridge straight up (they base out, no angle created).
- No lateral drive (you stay on your back or give them mount).
- Poor arrival (loose knees or no underhook = they recover guard or reverse).
- Using arms only for the bridge (gasses you, no power).

## Key Principles
- **Strong arm frame + elbow flare for space (FM6QISFsZeU / GB W06)**: The opening that makes the bridge possible. Same cue as every card in the W06 cluster and the mount bridge escapes (W04).
- **Head position high and outside**: The safety that lets you bridge without getting choked. Universal for all back and turtle work in Week 6.
- **Bridge + lateral hip drive (up and to the side)**: Not a straight bridge. Up + angled drive using legs and base creates the angle to roll to side mount or guard. Identical to the mount headlock bridge (W04-A2) and turtle recoveries.
- **Weight through legs and frames**: Power from the lower body and posted base. Frames only create space and direct the roll.
- **Frame clearance + underhook on arrival**: Use the frames to clear their grip as you roll, then secure underhook + tight knees so they cannot easily undo the escape.
- **Consistent GB language across back and mount escapes**: Frame/elbow space, head high, bridge + lateral hip drive, weight through base, safe arrival control. Unifies W06 (B2/B3) with W04 mount escapes and A2/A3 turtle recoveries.
- **Reaction-based**: Use their back control pressure — the harder they ride or choke, the more space your frames create for the bridge and roll.

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate / Curriculum Priority (per GB Greensboro rule):**
- Primary: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FM6QISFsZeU — Frame + elbow space + head high + lateral hip drive applied to back bridge escapes. GB Week 6 curriculum (gbcentennial.com + institute.graciebarra.com detailed recovery paths).
- Supporting: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PYk5Ei2IZBk — Back engagement framing, posture, and bridge/hip escape mechanics.
- Cross-ref the mount bridge escapes from Wave 2.7 (TGeJGo1DbgY Scottsdale Week 4 headlock bridge + 38bQ2vPsa-Q choke bridge) for the shared bridge + frame + hip drive family.

Cross-references: Drill B2 with B3 (the avoiding-mount variation), the full W06 cluster (A1–A3), and the mount bridge escapes (W04-A2/A3/B2). The back bridge escape is the direct counterpart to the mount bridge work.

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- Elbow flare frame in back control (space vs choke).
- Head high during the bridge.
- Explosive bridge + lateral hip drive (angled arrow).
- Frame clearance and roll to side mount (underhook arrival).
- Contrast: straight bridge (failure) vs lateral drive (success).

**Hand Placement & References**  
Elbow flare frames for space and choke defense, constant head high/outside, explosive bridge + lateral (not straight) hip drive powered by legs and base, frame clearance on the roll, and underhook + tight knees on arrival in side mount are the details from the GB W06 sources. These unify B2 with B3, A1–A3, and the W04 mount bridge escapes. Drill the full back bridge escape family together with the turtle recoveries and mount bridges.

## Personal Cues & Notes

GB1-W06-B3 - Bridge Escape from the Back.body:
## Concept

# Bridge Escape from the Back Avoiding the Mount + Recover Guard

**Position:** Back Control  
**Category:** Defense  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 6 B3  
**Confidence:** 4/5

From back control, you use the same strong frames, head high safety, and lateral hip drive as B2, but with the specific goal of avoiding giving the opponent mount during the escape and instead recovering directly to guard (or half guard). This is the "bridge to guard" variation that prevents the common mistake of bridging into mount.

GB1 Week 6 teaches B2 (to side mount) and B3 (avoid mount, to guard) as paired options so students can choose the right recovery based on the opponent's reaction and base.

## Setup

You are on your back or side with opponent on your back (hooks, seatbelt, or riding). They are threatening chokes or flattening you. Bridging straight up or in the wrong direction risks them taking mount as you roll.

- Goal: Frames to create space and protect the neck, bridge + lateral hip drive that specifically avoids the mount path, recover to closed guard or half guard while keeping head safe and establishing control.
- Paired with B2 for complete back escape options.

Wikilink connections:
- Part of the complete Week 6 family: [[GB1-W06-A1 - Ground Headlock Escape Pushing Head Away + Armbar]], [[GB1-W06-A2 - Recover Guard from Rear Turtle Position]], [[GB1-W06-A3 - Recover Guard from Front Turtle Position]], [[GB1-W06-B1 - Headlock Escape Taking the Back + Shoulder Lock]], [[GB1-W06-B2 - Bridge Escape from the Back + Side Mount]].
- Shares bridge + frame + hip drive with [[GB1-W04-A2 - Escape Mount with Headlock Using Bridge]] and [[GB1-W04-B2 - Escape Mount with Elbows (Opponent Won't Open Knee) + Recover Full Guard]] (the mount-side counterparts).
- Connects to turtle recoveries (A2/A3) and side control defense.

## Execution

### Frame + Head Safety (Same Foundation)
1. Immediate elbow-flared two-arm frame against their chest/arm/hip. Create space, stop the flatten, protect the neck.
2. Head high and outside. Chin tucked. Deny all chokes.

### Bridge + Lateral Drive Avoiding Mount Path
1. With frames in place, bridge explosively (hips up and angled) while driving laterally in the direction that rolls you toward your guard (not toward their mount).
2. The key angle: drive your hips up and to the side in a way that your body turns away from their base, landing you on your side or hip with them still on or sliding off to the side, not on top in mount.
3. Use frames to push their arm or hook clear as you roll. Establish underhook or collar tie immediately.
4. Recover to closed guard (or half guard) facing them, knees tight, posture safe. Head remains high and protected.

### Safe Guard Recovery
1. Once in guard, immediately establish collar or head control and posture so they cannot easily pass or strike.
2. If they are fast, accept half guard and work the recovery from there (see guard pull and half guard cards).

### Hand Placement & References
- **Elbow flare frame**: Same space and choke defense as B2 and the turtle recoveries.
- **Head high/outside**: Universal safety. Critical during the angled bridge to avoid chokes as you roll to guard.
- **Lateral hip drive angled to guard (not mount)**: The specific B3 variation. Up + side in the direction that turns your body away from their base so you land in guard, not mount.
- **Frame clearance + underhook on arrival**: Push their grip away and secure underhook as you recover guard.
- **Guard posture on landing**: Knees tight, collar control, head safe. Do not land flat and let them pass.

## Common Mistakes
- Bridging in the direction that gives them mount (the most common error on this escape).
- Weak frames (choke lands or they flatten you).
- Head drops inside during the angled bridge (choke or they keep top control).
- No underhook on guard arrival (they pass easily).
- Landing flat in half guard without posture (they set up knee on belly or pass).
- Using only arms for the bridge (no power, they base out).

## Key Principles
- **Strong arm frame + elbow flare for space (FM6QISFsZeU / GB W06)**: The foundation for every back and turtle escape in Week 6. Same opening as B2 and A1–A3.
- **Head position high and outside**: The safety cue that lets you bridge and roll without getting choked. Universal for the W06 family.
- **Bridge + lateral hip drive angled to recover guard (avoid mount path)**: The B3-specific variation. Choose the drive direction that turns you away from their base so you land in guard, not mount. This is the "avoid the mount" detail.
- **Weight through legs and frames**: Power from lower body and base. Frames create space and clear the path.
- **Frame clearance + underhook on guard arrival**: Push their grip away and secure control so the recovery actually sticks and they cannot immediately pass or take mount.
- **Consistent GB Week 6 language**: Frame/elbow space, head high, lateral hip drive (angled for guard vs mount), weight through base, safe control. Unifies B3 with B2 (side mount option), A1–A3 (turtle), and the W04 mount bridge escapes.
- **Reaction-based + choice**: Use their back pressure, then choose B2 (side mount) or B3 (guard) based on their base and your angle. Both are valid; B3 specifically defeats the "bridge into mount" mistake.

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate / Curriculum Priority (per GB Greensboro rule):**
- Primary: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FM6QISFsZeU — Frame + elbow space + head high + lateral hip drive for back bridge escapes that avoid mount and recover guard. GB Week 6 curriculum (gbcentennial.com + institute.graciebarra.com detailed paths for frames, hip insertion, recovery options).
- Supporting: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PYk5Ei2IZBk — Back engagement framing and safe transitions to guard.
- Cross-ref the mount bridge escapes (Wave 2.7 TGeJGo1DbgY and 38bQ2vPsa-Q) for the shared bridge + frame + hip drive family and the specific angle choices that avoid mount.

Cross-references: Drill B3 with B2 (paired back bridge options), the full W06 cluster (A1–A3, B1), and the mount bridge escapes (W04-A2/A3/B2). The choice between B2 (side mount) and B3 (guard) is a key decision point students must own.

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- Elbow flare frame in back control.
- Head high during angled bridge.
- Lateral hip drive angled specifically to guard (arrow showing turn away from their base).
- Frame clearance and arrival in closed guard (underhook + tight knees).
- Contrast: bridge path that gives mount (bad) vs angled path to guard (good).

**Hand Placement & References**  
Elbow flare frames, constant head high/outside, lateral hip drive angled to land in guard (not mount), frame clearance, underhook on arrival, and immediate guard posture are the details from the GB W06 sources. These unify B3 with B2 and the entire W06 family, and connect to the mount bridge work (W04) where the same angle awareness prevents giving guard or side control. Drill B2 + B3 together as the complete back bridge escape curriculum.

## Personal Cues & Notes
